1:
The chapter opens the roll of the temple porters, and the witnesses set the dignity of the office before they begin to number the men. The reformed John Gill defines the work plainly: these were the doorkeepers, whose business was to open and shut the doors of the house of God, to keep every impure and improper person from entering, to prevent any of the vessels being carried out, and to guard against tumults and riots about the courts; and David, he notes, divided them into classes like the singers, that they might take their turns in waiting. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the heading — literally, he says, it reads "as to courses, to porters" — and reckons that as many as four thousand Levites were set apart for this function by the king's orders, citing 1 Chr 23:6 and 1 Chr 23:25. He fixes the lineage of the first captain: to the Korhites, the sons of Korah, belonged Meshelemiah the son of Kore, a man called Shelemiah at v.14 and Shallum at 1 Chr 9:19; and he warns that the "Asaph" named here is not the Gershonite chief musician but an abbreviation of Ebiasaph, the Korhites being a Kohathite stock.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the humble office into a meditation. The porters, he says, guarded all the avenues that led to the sanctuary, and attended the outer gates not only for state but for service — to direct those going up to worship, to encourage the timorous, to send back the stranger and the unclean, and to keep out thieves and every enemy of the house of God. In allusion to this office, he adds, ministers are said to have the keys of the kingdom of heaven committed to them, that they may admit and exclude according to the law of Christ, as at Matthew 16:19. Henry marks too that several of these men are noticed as mighty men of valour, as strong men, as able men, and one as a wise counsellor — for whatever service God calls men to, he either finds them fit or makes them so.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the architecture the chapter will fill in: there were four classes of porters, each belonging to one of the four gates that opened to the four cardinal points — the eastern falling to Shelemiah, the northern to Zechariah, the southern to Obed-edom, and the western to Shuppim and Hosah. These, he says, were the captains of the porter-bands, and probably a thousand men served under each, making up the four thousand of 1 Chr 23:5. The voices do not divide; together they frame a guard of honour set over the doors of God, and open a register in which even the doorkeeping is entailed from father to son.
2:
The register turns to the household of the first captain, and the witnesses read the seven sons of Meshelemiah as seven living branches of a single guild. The reformed John Gill notes simply that Zechariah was the firstborn, followed by six more who are named in their order. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses beyond the bare list to its meaning: "and Meshelemiah had sons," he renders, and he takes the seven "sons" of Meshelemiah — the same man he elsewhere calls Shallum — to represent not merely seven individuals but seven guilds of porters descended from him. The names are the seed of a company, and the firstborn Zechariah is the same of whom more will be heard when the lot sends him to the north gate as "a wise counsellor" at v.14.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, set the family in its tribal place. Meshelemiah, like Obed-edom after him, belonged to the Kohathite family of the Korahites; and the whole body of doorkeepers, they observe from v.19, was drawn from the Korahites and the Merarites alone. They mark the care of the enumeration — first the sons here, then the grandsons in the verses following — and note the Hebrew idiom by which the verb "was born" stands in the singular though a plural offspring follows. The point that the witnesses share is that this opening roster is no idle antiquarianism: each name answers to a course of guards, so that the seven sons of Meshelemiah are in effect the roll-call of a standing watch.

There is no disagreement here, only a division of labour among the expositors. Gill gives the plain succession of birth; Ellicott gives the institutional sense, that these households became the porter-guilds; Keil and Delitzsch give the genealogical frame, tying Meshelemiah to Korah and Kohath and forward to the subscription of v.19, where only two of Levi's three houses are named as keepers of the gates. Together they teach the reader to hear in a list of seven names the ordered ministry of a temple, in which even the doorkeeping was numbered with the same exactness as the priesthood and the song, and handed down as a sacred trust from one generation to the next.
3:
Only one witness pauses over this verse, and he pauses only to weigh the names. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, running down the roll of Meshelemiah's sons, stops at the sixth and seventh — Jehohanan and Elioenai — and unfolds the theology folded into their Hebrew. Jehohanan, he explains, means "Jah bestowed," or "the LORD hath been gracious," and is the full, uncontracted form of the name that later shortens to Johanan, and later still, in the mouth of the New Testament, to John. The name is a small confession: the child is received as a gift graciously given.

Elioenai he parses with equal care, reading it as "mine eyes are toward Jehovah" — the very posture of the pilgrim who lifts up his eyes to the God enthroned in the heavens, as in Psalms 123:1 — and he notes that it too is the full form of a name elsewhere abbreviated, the Elioenai of 1 Chr 3:24. The observation is characteristic of Ellicott's whole handling of these genealogies: the names are not dead syllables but living testimonies, each carrying a scrap of a family's faith. Of Elam the fifth, named alongside them, not even Ellicott finds anything to say, and the rest of the witnesses pass all three in silence, the roster running on too quickly for a full treatment of every son.

Yet Ellicott's lingering supplies what the bare list withholds. A doorkeeper named "mine eyes are toward Jehovah" bore in his own name the disposition proper to his office, watching Godward at the gate of the house of God; while his brother Jehohanan stood as a standing memorial that the LORD had been gracious to the house of Meshelemiah. The pattern Ellicott exposes — the long, devout form preserved here where later scribes would clip it — is itself a quiet argument that the Chronicler drew these names from an old and careful source. So even this thinnest of verses, where a single expositor speaks and the others keep silence, is not empty: it shows how the piety of the porters was written into the very names their fathers gave them, and how the roll of the guard, read closely, is a record of grace acknowledged and of eyes turned upward toward the God whose gates these men were set to keep.
4:
The roll passes to a household already dear to the reader's memory, and the witnesses greet it with warmth. The reformed John Gill recalls at once who this Obed-edom was — the man at whose house the ark had lodged before David brought it up to Zion — and notes that his sons became porters like himself, eight of them here set down according to their birth. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the genealogical knot and unties it: Obed-edom, called a son of Jeduthun at 1 Chr 16:38, is not thereby made a Merarite, for this Jeduthun was not the Merarite chief musician but a Korhite; the porter Obed-edom belongs, with Meshelemiah, to the Kohathite Korahites of v.1 and the subscription of v.19.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the memory into a lesson in reward. This, he says, was the very man who entertained the ark with reverence and cheerfulness; and see how he was rewarded for it. His sons, who had faithfully attended the ark in their own house, are now called to attend it in God's house — for he that is trusty in little shall be trusted with more, and he that keeps God's ordinances in his own tent is fit to have the custody of them in God's tabernacle. Henry hears the principle sounded in David's own confession, "I have kept thy law," which the psalmist traces to the keeping of God's precepts in Psalms 119:56: the honour of standing at the sanctuary gate grows out of the humbler faithfulness that first sheltered the ark under a private roof.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement carries a quiet theology of the household. Obed-edom the Gittite, once merely the keeper of an ark left on his hands after Uzzah's death, becomes the father of a guild of temple guards; the man who welcomed God's presence into his home is repaid by having his whole family drawn into God's service. Gill fixes the history, Ellicott the pedigree, Henry the moral — and between them they make the mere notice "the sons of Obed-edom" open upon one of the tenderest providences in the chapter, that a reverence shown to the ark in obscurity should be answered by a standing place at the doors of the house of the LORD for generations of his seed.
5:
The register pauses on a clause of pure gratitude — "for God blessed him" — and the witnesses gather to read the blessing off the very names of the sons. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott shows that Obed-edom's eight sons are, one and all, testimonies to his thankful recognition of divine favour, echoing the word of 1 Chr 13:14 that "God blessed the house of Obed-edom." He reads them off like a litany: Shemaiah, "Jah hath heard" the prayer for offspring; Jehozabad, "Jah hath bestowed"; Joah, "Jah is a kinsman"; Sacar, "reward," the very word of Genesis 15:1; Nethaneel, "God hath given"; Ammiel, "a kinsman is God"; Issachar, "there is a reward"; and Peulthai, "the recompense of Jah." The father wrote his thanks into his children.

The wesleyan-arminian voices press the ground of the blessing. Adam Clarke, following the Targum, ties it directly to the ark — God blessed Obed-edom because of the ark of the LORD which was in his house, granting him the honour of seeing his children and grandchildren, even fourscore and two, become masters among the Levites, though only sixty-two are counted at v.8. John Wesley makes the same point a maxim: the increase and building up of families is owing to the divine blessing, and it is a great blessing to have many children when, like these, they are eminent in the service of God. The reward answers exactly to the reverence Obed-edom had shown the ark.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already drawn the thread out: Obed-edom had eight sons, he says, for God blessed him, and the multiplying of a household is a gift of heaven. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, keep the account in order, gathering the eight sons and the grandsons of the following verses into the sixty-two able men of Obed-edom's family, and noting how the singular "mighty man of strength" stands in apposition to the whole company. The witnesses do not divide. Where a colder reader might see only a list, they hear a doxology: a man who once trembled to have the ark under his roof is crowned with a household so numerous and so useful that its names become a hymn of thanks, and the reader is taught that the surest way to a blessed house is to make room in it for the presence of God.
6:
The record notes that the sons of Shemaiah "ruled throughout the house of their father, for they were mighty men of valour," and the witnesses fasten on that martial word. The reformed John Gill marks only that Shemaiah here is the firstborn of Obed-edom, already named. But the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws the sharp point: these men were not doorkeepers in the ordinary sense alone, but a military guard for the gates — and perhaps, he suggests, it is in this sense alone that we are to understand their office. The porter was a soldier at his post.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the English and deepens the sense. "That ruled throughout the house of their father" is better rendered "the lords of their clan," for the word translated "that ruled" is not a verb at all but an abstract noun, mimshal — like our word "the authorities" or "the government" — a term that occurs elsewhere only in Daniel 11:3 and its neighbour verse. These were the ruling heads of their household, and the added phrase "mighty men of valour" he glosses by his note at 1 Chr 9:13, where the same strength is required of the temple's servants.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley explains why the clause of valour is sounded again and again through the chapter: the porter's office required both strength and courage. They had to shut the massive doors of the temple, one of which, he says, was so great and weighty that in the second temple it took twenty men to open and shut it; they had to keep the guard, to bar the unclean and forbidden, to prevent or suppress any tumult in the courts, to keep the treasures named at v.20, and at last to serve as officers and judges over Israel and to manage every matter pertaining to God and the affairs of the king. So the witnesses converge on a single portrait, differing only in emphasis. Gill supplies the name, Clarke the soldierly character, Ellicott the grammar that makes these men the lords of their clan, and Wesley the concrete labour that demanded such men — heavy gates, watchful nights, and the guarding of a holy place. The doorkeepers of God's house were chosen for their strength because the house had real enemies, and the guard set upon it was no ceremonial fiction but a company of valiant men.
7:
The verse names the grandsons — the sons of Shemaiah, with their brethren Elihu and Semachiah — and the witnesses find the Hebrew here so bruised that most of their labour is textual repair. The reformed John Gill passes over the difficulty, noting only that this Shemaiah is the firstborn of Obed-edom already mentioned. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, by contrast, stops at every seam. The pair "Obed, Elzabad" he judges probably corrupt, for the connecting "and" that joins the other names is wanting between them; "Obed-Elzabad" may well be the wreck of a single name — perhaps Obed-el, or Abdiel, a form the Syriac and Arabic seem to preserve, though those versions give only six names here where the Septuagint gives eight.

Ellicott finds the fracture again in the next clause. "Whose brethren were strong men" reads, in the Hebrew, "his brethren," and once more the conjunction has dropped out; he would restore it and read, "And his brethren, sons of strength, Elihu and Semachiah." The verse, in his handling, is a small laboratory of the copyist's slips — a lost letter here, a fallen "and" there — and he refuses to smooth them over, preferring to let the reader see the text as it actually stands and to weigh the ancient versions against it.

What looks at first like dry emendation carries a real payoff, and Ellicott himself points toward it. If "Obed-Elzabad" is indeed one composite name, like Obed-edom itself, then Shemaiah had five sons and not six; and that single correction, he will argue at v.12, is exactly what brings the total number of porter-courses out at the significant figure of twenty-four, matching the twenty-four courses of the singers. So the two witnesses divide their work without contending: Gill anchors the man in his family, while Ellicott counts the letters, trusting that the recovery of the true reading is not pedantry but the key to the pattern the Chronicler intended — a guard ordered, like the priests and the musicians, into four-and-twenty companies. The very disagreement of the copies — eight names in the Greek, six in the Syriac and Arabic — becomes, in his hands, further evidence that a name has been split or fused, and a reason to weigh the versions rather than trust the received count. The bruised verse turns out to guard a number.
8:
The record now sums the house of Obed-edom — "all these of the sons of Obed-edom... threescore and two" — and the witnesses read the total both as arithmetic and as grammar. The reformed John Gill notes that the sixty-two comprehend not the sons only but the grandsons too, the whole three generations gathered into one able company. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, give the same reckoning: the entire number of doorkeepers of Obed-edom's family — his sons and his brethren alike — was sixty-two, able men with strength for the service; and they resolve a small oddity of the Hebrew, that the singular "man of might" follows a plural, by taking it in apposition to the "all" at the head of the verse, as though one repeated "every" before it.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, presses the same grammar further. "Able men," he says, is in the original a singular collective — "men of power" — and "for strength" is literally "in the strength," that is, in ability. The number he reads as a distinct sentence: "there were sixty and two belonging to Obed-edom." He suspects that the little word "every" has fallen out before "man of might," as it stands in the parallel phrase of 1 Chr 10:12, and would render the whole: "all these were of the sons of Obed-edom; they and their sons and their brethren, every man of power in the strength for service."

Ellicott closes with a comparison that lifts the roster out of mere counting. The "sons and brethren" of the porters, he observes, may be set beside the "sons and brethren" of the musicians at 1 Chr 25:9, where the same language orders the twenty-four courses of the singers. The hint is that the doorkeepers are being numbered on the same principle as the temple choir — households reckoned as guilds, sons and kinsmen together forming a course of service. The witnesses do not divide; between Gill's plain tally and the grammatical-historical care of Keil, Delitzsch, and Ellicott, the verse yields a picture of a single family swollen by God's blessing into sixty-two men of strength, every one of them fit for the service of the gates, and all of them counted with the same exactness the Chronicler gives to those who ministered in song.
9:
A short verse gives Meshelemiah's total — "sons and brethren, strong men, eighteen" — and the witnesses turn it into a lesson in the Chronicler's bookkeeping. The reformed John Gill does the sum: since Meshelemiah had seven sons, named earlier, he must here be credited with eleven brethren or kinsmen besides, the two together making the eighteen. The number is not a lump but a family reckoned by its parts.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, lift the eye from the single house to the whole guard. Meshelemiah's eighteen, Obed-edom's sixty-two of v.8, and Hosah's thirteen of v.11, taken together, make ninety-three — and these ninety-three, they explain, are to be regarded as the heads of the four thousand doorkeepers appointed by David. They are careful with a seeming discrepancy: the two hundred and twelve porters numbered at 1 Chr 9:22 belong most probably to a different time, and the sixty-eight of 1 Chr 16:38 to yet another moment in the history, so that the figures are not rival totals but snapshots of a growing institution. Bertheau's further remark, that sixty-eight are reckoned in the earlier notice, they weigh in the same spirit, treating the shifting numbers not as errors to be forced into one sum but as records of the same office caught at successive stages of its history.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, reads the verse as a supplement that reaches back to v.2, filling in the strength of Meshelemiah's house after the long excursus on Obed-edom. Adding the eighteen of Meshelemiah to the sixty-two of Obed-edom, he counts eighty families among the Korhite porters alone, before the Merarite Hosah is brought in. The witnesses do not divide; they simply hand the reader a set of nested totals — seven sons within eighteen kinsmen within eighty Korhite families within ninety-three chief men within four thousand Levites — and in that patient nesting the point emerges. The doorkeeping of the temple was no casual duty flung to whoever stood near the gate, but a vast, ordered ministry, numbered from the single son up to the whole army of guards, every man known and placed, so that the house of God should never want for a watch.
10:
With Hosah the register crosses from one Levitical house to another — "Hosah, of the children of Merari" — and the witnesses mark the moment the Merarites enter the guard. The reformed John Gill recalls that Hosah had been a fellow porter of Obed-edom's at the ark, and here has sons of his own to enroll. But the verse's real interest lies in its parenthesis: Simri was made the chief "though he was not the firstborn," and around that clause the witnesses gather.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, explain the circumstance plainly. Hosah made Shimri head of his Merarite doorkeepers because there was no firstborn — that is, the firstborn son had died without leaving descendants, so that no family sprung from Hosah held the natural claim of the birthright, and the headship had to be assigned on other grounds. All the sons and brothers of Hosah, they add, were thirteen. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott offers the alternative: it may mean either that the eldest family had died out, or that none of these families could prove its seniority over the rest — either way, the primogeniture had lapsed.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry agree on the principle the case establishes. Wesley: Hosah made Simri the chief not in inheriting the estate — that the law forbade — but in this service, for which he was better qualified than his elder brother. Henry says the same of it, observing that one here, though not the first-born, was made chief, perhaps because the eldest was weak or because this one was very excellent; and he is careful that this could not touch the inheritance, since the law of Deuteronomy 21:16 forbade a father to prefer a younger son's birthright, but touched only an office which required personal fitness. So the witnesses converge on a quiet but pointed doctrine. In the ordinary descent of property, birth ruled and could not be set aside; but in the service of God, gift and fitness might lift a younger man over his elder, for the guarding of the house demanded qualities that no order of birth could guarantee. The Merarite Simri stands, at the head of his clan, as a small monument to the principle that God's work is entrusted to the fit.
11:
The verse completes Hosah's house — "Hilkiah the second, Tebaliah the third, Zechariah the fourth; all the sons and brethren of Hosah were thirteen" — and the witnesses close the roster of the porters with a last count. The reformed John Gill breaks the thirteen into its parts: four sons, he reckons, and nine brethren or kinsmen, the two together making the thirteen of the Merarite clan.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, use this closing number to draw the whole enumeration to a head. With Hosah's thirteen now added to Meshelemiah's eighteen and Obed-edom's sixty-two, the three doorkeeper-houses stand complete at ninety-three chief men; and they read the verse as the pivot into what follows, where these named heads are shown to be, precisely, the classes or divisions of the porters — "to these classes of doorkeepers, to the heads of the men, were committed the watches, in common with their brethren, to serve in the house of the LORD." The list of names, that is, has all along been a list of courses.

The witnesses do not divide, and the quiet completeness of the moment is itself the point. Two Levitical houses have been numbered, the Korahite Meshelemiah and Obed-edom, and now the Merarite Hosah; between them they furnish the ninety-three heads under whom the four thousand doorkeepers of David's ordering would serve. Gill's small arithmetic — four sons, nine kinsmen — and Keil and Delitzsch's larger reckoning meet on the same conviction that pervades the chapter: that nothing in the service of the temple was left to chance or to crowd, but every guard had his household, every household its head, and every head his place in a total that the Chronicler could name to the man. The thirteen of Hosah, small as the number is, are set down with the same deliberateness as the sixty-two of Obed-edom, for in this reckoning no house is too slight to be counted, and the least of the Merarite doorkeepers has his name preserved as surely as the greatest of the Korahites. The roster of the gates ends here, thirteen added to eighty, ninety-three in all, and the record turns from who the porters were to where each was to stand.
12:
The verse gathers the foregoing names under a single institutional word — "among these were the divisions of the porters" — and the witnesses agree that here the list is declared to be a system. The reformed John Gill reads it so: those already named were the principal men of the porters, among whom there was a division into classes or courses, in which they served week by week in their turn. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same, that these had "wards" answerably to their brethren the other Levites, who were divided into twenty-four courses, as the priests and the singers were.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes that parallel the key to the whole passage, and works it out with the patience of an accountant. This verse, he says, makes it evident that the names of the preceding verses represent the courses of the porters; and just as the twenty-four sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun stood for the twenty-four courses of musicians in 1 Chr 25, a like classification is to be looked for here. He counts: seven sons of Meshelemiah, eight sons of Obed-edom, four sons of Hosah — nineteen heads in all — to which must be added the sons of Shemaiah. As the text stands these appear to be six, giving an awkward twenty-five; but if, as he argued at v.7, "Obed-Elzabad" is a single composite name, then Shemaiah had five sons, and the total comes out at the very number expected — twenty-four courses of warders.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, confirm the sense of the Hebrew without pressing the arithmetic so far: the phrase means that to these classes of doorkeepers, to the heads of the men, the watches were committed, in common with their brethren, to minister in the house of the LORD. The witnesses thus converge on a striking design. The guarding of the temple was ordered exactly as its worship was — into four-and-twenty companies, so that priest, singer, and porter alike served the house on a single rotating plan. Ellicott's earlier labour over a lost conjunction is here vindicated: the correction that seemed mere pedantry restores the symmetry the Chronicler intended, and the doors of God are shown to be kept by the same sacred number that ordered the sacrifice and the song.
13:
The stations are now assigned, and the manner of the assignment arrests the witnesses: "they cast lots, as well the small as the great." The reformed John Gill reads the purpose simply — the lot was to determine at which gate each should stand, this man here and that man there. The wesleyan-arminian voices press the equity of it. Adam Clarke observes that none of these captains or their companies was permitted to choose which gate to guard; each took his appointment by lot. John Wesley makes the same point sharper: the times and places of their service were determined not by age or dignity but merely by lot, that it might be known to whom the care of each gate was committed.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott insists on the phrase "small and great alike," rendering it "senior and junior," and draws the doctrine out: the posts of the porters were assigned by lot without distinction of rank between the various families, so that the humblest house had the same claim on the gate of honour as the greatest. He then turns to the shape of the building the lots divided. The sanctuary was built square with the four points of the compass, with a gate on each side; the orientation of temples, he notes, was the rule among the ancient Semites, and the importance they attached to the cardinal points is seen in the old style of the Babylonian and Assyrian kings as "king of the four quarters" of heaven.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, describe the casting in the same terms — the small as the great, the younger as the older, drew lots according to their fathers'-houses, "for door and door," that is, for each door of the four sides of the temple, whose sides were made to answer to the points of the compass. The witnesses do not divide. What they see together in this verse is a deliberate refusal of favouritism at the very heart of the temple's order: the lot, which takes the decision out of human hands and lays it in God's, levels senior and junior, great house and small, and posts each guard where heaven, not ambition, would have him. The four-square house of God, opening to the four winds, is guarded by a company from which rank has been, for this purpose, entirely erased.
14:
The first lot falls, and it falls eastward — "to Shelemiah" — and the witnesses treat the east gate as the place of honour. The reformed John Gill identifies Shelemiah with Meshelemiah, now posted at the eastern gate, later called the gate Shushan because the city of Shushan was portrayed upon it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the assignment as no accident of chance but a fitness confirmed by lot: the courses of the sons of Shelemiah — the Meshelemiah of v.2, the Shallum of 1 Chr 9:19 — received the post of honour on the east side of the sanctuary, the side toward the sunrise and the main approach.

Then comes a touch that the witnesses will not pass over. The lot for the north fell to Zechariah, Shelemiah's firstborn, whom the text calls "a wise counsellor." Keil and Delitzsch note the title and confess they do not know why it is given — "for reasons unknown to us" — but they record it faithfully, that the north side fell to this sagacious son. Ellicott makes far more of it. The Hebrew, he says, reads "Zechariah his son, counselling with sagacity," and he takes the plural verb "they cast lots" to suit Zechariah as the name of a clan or guild rather than a single man. But it is the epithet itself that seizes him: this little touch, he says, is obviously a mark of truth. The Chronicler could have had no motive for so characterising a mere warder of the temple unless he had found the description in some older source, of which he has given us only extracts.

So the witnesses land, without contending, on two things at once. Gill and Ellicott together fix the geography and the dignity — the east gate, the post of honour, given to the senior house; the north to its wise-counselling firstborn. And Ellicott adds the quiet apologetic value of the phrase: that a genealogist inventing a list would never have paused to call a gatekeeper "a wise counsellor," so that the very oddity of the compliment vouches for the antiquity and honesty of the record. The lot that seems to scatter men at random is seen, on this reading, to place a discerning man where discernment was needed, and to preserve, in a single unexpected word, the memory of a real person long since dead.
15:
The southern lot falls to Obed-edom, and with it the charge of a building the witnesses labour to identify — "the house of Asuppim." The reformed John Gill sets the location, noting that on the south side stood, in later times, two gates called the gates of Huldah. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, define the structure: Beth-ha-asuppim, called for brevity "Asuppim" at v.17, was a house of collections or provisions — compare Nehemiah 12:25 — a building used for storing the temple goods, set near the southern door in the outer court, and probably furnished with two entrances, since two guard-stations are assigned to it at v.17.

The witnesses divide, mildly, over what the name means. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees with Keil and Delitzsch that the word occurs only here and in Nehemiah 12:25, and denotes collections — stores of provisions and material for the temple and its ministers — so that Beth-ha-asuppim is simply the storehouse or magazine; nothing more, he says, can be known of it, and he notes that the Vulgate went astray in taking it for a "council of elders," confusing it with a different Hebrew word. The wesleyan-arminian witnesses lean the other way. Adam Clarke allows the plainer sense — the house of the collections, where the porters' supplies or the offerings for the priests and Levites were laid up — but John Wesley prefers to render it "of gatherings," and thinks it probably named from the assembly of the elders, who met there to consult about the affairs of the temple.

So a genuine, if small, disagreement stands over this word. Was the house of Asuppim a granary — a storehouse of the temple's provisions, as the grammatical-historical witnesses and Clarke maintain — or a council-chamber, a place of gathering for the elders, as Wesley and the old Vulgate would have it? The witnesses do not resolve it, and cannot, for the term is nearly a hapax; but the majority weight of the reading falls on the storehouse, a place of stores requiring, like a treasury, the vigilance of armed doorkeepers. Either way the point of the verse holds: Obed-edom's house is set to the south, and given charge not merely of a gate but of a building where the goods of the sanctuary — provisions or property or both — were kept under guard.
16:
The western lot brings the chapter's thorniest text — "to Shuppim and Hosah the lot came forth westward, with the gate Shallecheth" — and the witnesses part company at the very first name. The reformed John Gill takes "Shuppim" as a real if obscure man, of whom no mention has been made before, and locates his and Hosah's charge at the gates of the western wall. But the grammatical-historical witnesses will not have him. Keil and Delitzsch call the word unintelligible, and judge that it crept into the text merely by a repetition of the last two syllables of the preceding "Asuppim"; Charles Ellicott says the same more flatly — no such warder as Shuppim occurs in the roster of verses 1 through 11, and the name is almost certainly a mistaken echo of "Asuppim," a slip as old as the Vulgate. Read simply, they urge, "to Hosah the lot fell westward."

On the gate itself the witnesses come nearer agreement. Ellicott derives Shallecheth from a root meaning "casting down" — the felling of a tree in Isaiah 6:13 — and so would identify it with a "refuse" or "rubbish" gate, comparing the dung-gate of Nehemiah 3:13, though he grants an objection: this gate faced the highway that went up from the lower city to the temple, and so might rather take its name from the steep drop down to the town. The wesleyan-arminian voices settle on the same sense: Adam Clarke calls it the gate of the projections, probably that through which the offal of the temple was carried out; John Wesley, that the ashes and filth were cast out on that side, the most convenient because it was a private quarter, the great approaches lying on the other sides.

A last phrase, "ward against ward," divides them once more. Ellicott offers two readings: that Hosah had to guard two posts, the western gate and the gate Shallecheth opposite it in the western wall — a sense the Septuagint and Vulgate support with "guard over against guard" — or else that the words describe all four stations, "ward like ward," post answering to post. Wesley takes this broader view, that as one gate stood over against another, west against east and north against south, so one ward stood over against another. Keil and Delitzsch add only that the "ascending highway" was the road climbing from the lower city to the loftier temple site. The witnesses leave two knots half-tied — a name that may be a scribe's stammer, and a phrase that may mean a double post or a general symmetry — but agree that Hosah kept the western side, by the gate through which the refuse of the holy house went out.
17:
The verse counts the daily guard at each side — "eastward were six Levites, northward four... southward four... toward Asuppim two and two" — and the witnesses attend to both the numbers and their meaning. The reformed John Gill explains the surplus in the east: the eastern gate, being the way of entrance into the temple, required more porters than the rest. The wesleyan-arminian voices say the same: Adam Clarke supposes more guards were set there because it was the most frequented, six at this gate against four at each of the others; John Wesley, that being the chief gate of the temple it required a better guard.

The grammatical-historical witnesses tally the whole watch. Charles Ellicott renders it exactly — to the east six, to the north four for the day, to the south four for the day, and at the stores two apiece, the magazine having, as it seems, two doors with two warders at each. Keil and Delitzsch add the west from v.18 and reach the sum: six east, four north, four south, four at the storehouse, and six on the west — twenty-four guard-stations to be occupied each day. Yet, they caution, more than twenty-four men were needed, since one man could not stand a whole day at his post but had to be relieved, and probably more than one watched at each station at once.

Here a quiet difference of judgment opens between the two grammatical-historical voices, to be pressed further at v.18. Keil and Delitzsch resist making too much of the figure: though the number twenty-four may seem to connect with the twenty-four divisions of the doorkeepers, they say, there is only a deceptive appearance of a connection, and the analogy of the priests and singers is no sufficient proof. Ellicott, by contrast, finds the recurrence of twenty-four too striking to dismiss, and will suggest that the daily warders represent chiefs with their companies. So the witnesses agree on the arithmetic while dividing on its significance. All grant that the east, as the gate of entrance and honour, was doubly manned; all grant that four-and-twenty stations were kept each day; but whether that number deliberately mirrors the twenty-four courses, or merely happens to fall out so, they leave as an open and honestly contested question.
18:
The tally of the west closes with a word no one can fully explain — "at Parbar westward, four at the causeway, and two at Parbar." The reformed John Gill reports the older guesses: the Jewish writers generally take Parbar for an outward place, but Dr. Lightfoot thought it the same as Parvar, a word signifying "suburbs," which would agree with Josephus's account of a western gate that led to the suburbs. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the philology, pointing to the plural Parwarim at 2 Kings 23:11, and confesses the meaning unknown; following Gesenius he prefers the spelling Parwar, answering to a Persian word for a "summer-house" — a building open to light and air — so that "the Parbar" would be a colonnade or cloister running round the temple court, from which the store-cells were entered, as in his note at 1 Chr 23:28. A Persian dictionary, he adds, gives fifteen variant forms of the word.

So a small disagreement stands over the place. Was Parbar a suburb or outer precinct, as Lightfoot and the older Jewish writers held, or an open gallery, a summer-colonnade of cells around the court, as Ellicott after Gesenius supposes? Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, leave the sense uncertain but keep the arithmetic clear: at the causeway — the ascending highway of v.16 — stood four, and at Parbar itself two, making six on the western side.

That western six lets Ellicott complete the count begun at v.17: adding the stations of the two verses, there were twenty-four warders on duty every day. The recurrence of the number, he grants, is curious, and its exact relation to the twenty-four classes of porters can hardly be determined — but it is likely, he thinks, that the twenty-four represent chiefs with their companies rather than single men, for twenty-four would be an insignificant fraction of the four thousand Levites of 1 Chr 23:6. The witnesses thus leave the reader with one word they cannot translate and one number they cannot quite account for, yet with the plan itself perfectly firm: a guard set at every side of the house, the obscure Parbar on the west manned like the rest, and the whole watch of the temple summed, day by day, at four-and-twenty posts.
19:
A subscription closes the whole section — "these are the divisions of the porters among the sons of Kore, and among the sons of Merari" — and the witnesses read it as the Chronicler's own summary of who was, and who was not, admitted to the guard. The reformed John Gill unfolds the genealogy compressed in the two names: among the Levites who descended from Kore, a grandson of Kohath, and from Merari, a brother of Kohath, the porters only were found; the doorkeeping was the peculiar charge of these two lines.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the negative implied in the positive. This concluding remark, he says, proves that only the Kohathite and Merarite divisions of Levi had any part in the duty of temple-warders; the Gershonites were not represented among the porters at all, as the opening and the roster of verses 1 and 10 had already shown. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, say only what the verse says — that this is the subscription in which it is again stated that the classes of doorkeepers were taken from the Korahites and the Merarites — content to let the Chronicler round off his own list. He could have left the two houses to be inferred from the roster, but he states them again in a closing line, that no reader might suppose the Gershonites shared a duty which was never theirs; the repetition is the deliberate seal of a record meaning to be exact about who stood at the doors of God.

The witnesses do not divide, and the quiet exactness of the notice is its own lesson. The three great houses of Levi — Gershon, Kohath, Merari — did not share the temple offices indiscriminately; each had its appointed work, and the keeping of the gates fell to two of them and not the third. Gill's care to trace Kore to Kohath and to set Merari as Kohath's brother, and Ellicott's insistence that the Gershonites are here silently excluded, meet on the same conviction that governs the whole chapter: that the service of God was ordered by descent and appointment, not by volunteering, so that a man knew both his house and his duty. The subscription seals the roster of the porters as it opened it — a guard drawn, by divine arrangement, from Korah and from Merari, and from these alone.
20:
The chapter turns from the gates to the vaults — "over the treasures of the house of God, and over the treasures of the dedicated things" — and the witnesses meet at once a textual puzzle and a rich theme. The verse names one Ahijah as overseer, but the grammatical-historical witnesses find the name doubtful. Charles Ellicott calls the whole opening strange — "and of the Levites, Ahijah" — since none but Levites have been in question, and objects that Ahijah appears nowhere among the names of 1 Chr 23:7 and following, stands alone without the family-reference every other steward is given, and needs only a single added letter to become "their brethren," which is in fact the reading of the Septuagint. He would render, "and the Levites their brethren were over the treasures" — that is, the Levites other than those already described. Keil and Delitzsch reach the same suspicion by the Masoretic accents, finding that "Ahijah" cannot be made to harmonize and that the bare "of the Levites" gives no clear sense. The reformed John Gill, keeping the received text, simply reads it of "the rest of the Levites" — the Gershonites, Amramites, Izharites, and Hebronites now to be enrolled.

On the treasures themselves the witnesses divide the labour helpfully. Ellicott and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley distinguish two funds: the treasures of the house of God — the ordinary revenues and stores, the legally prescribed contributions and special gifts of Exodus 30:11, Leviticus 27, and Numbers 18:16 — and the treasures of the dedicated things, the spoils described below. Adam Clarke, following Jarchi, marks the first as the place where the money was kept for the oblations of the temple.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the whole to its height. A great house, he says, cannot be kept without stores; much was spent daily on the altar — flour, wine, oil, salt, fuel, besides the lamps and the sacred vestments and utensils — and these were the treasures of the house of God, which typified the plenty there is in our heavenly Father's house, enough and to spare, for in Christ, the true temple, are hid the treasures of wisdom and knowledge and unsearchable riches. These treasures had their treasurers, he adds, set over them to keep them, that neither moth nor rust should corrupt them nor thieves break through and steal, and probably to keep the accounts. The witnesses do not divide on the substance: a doubtful name, two well-ordered funds, and behind the flour and oil a parable of the fulness of God.
21:
The record begins to name the treasurers, and it opens with the Gershonite branch — "the sons of Laadan" — over the treasures of the house of the LORD. The reformed John Gill fixes the man at once: this Laadan is the same as Libni, the elder line of the Gershonites under another form of his name. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, work patiently through the crabbed Hebrew, which packs a whole pedigree into a few words. The verse means, they explain, the sons of Laadan — that is, the sons of the Gershonite family belonging to Laadan, the heads of the fathers'-houses of Laadan the Gershonite — namely Jehieli, whose own sons Zetham and Joel were set over the treasures of the house of God.

The obscurity, Keil and Delitzsch show, is only compression, and it clears the moment one lays the verse beside 1 Chr 23:7, where the sons of Jehiel, the Jehielites, are traced to Laadan the older branch of the Gershonites. Read in that light, the tangled clauses are simply three successive definitions of one family: first the descent from Laadan, then the naming of its head Jehieli, then the appointment of his sons Zetham and Joel to the oversight of the temple's treasures.

The witnesses do not divide, and the very difficulty of the verse becomes an argument for its antiquity. No later hand inventing a list would have written so crabbedly; the knot is the knot of a genuine old record, faithfully copied though its idiom had grown strange. Gill's small equation of Laadan with Libni and Keil and Delitzsch's careful unpacking meet on the same point: that the first of the two treasuries, the ordinary stores of the house of God, was committed to a Gershonite house — the descendants of Laadan through Jehieli — while the dedicated spoils, as the following verses will show, were laid under the charge of another line entirely. Even the keeping of the temple's money was parcelled out by descent, one family for the household treasure and another for the consecrated, so that responsibility was fixed and no man's trust overlapped his brother's.
22:
The verse names the two Gershonite treasurers outright — "the sons of Jehieli; Zetham, and Joel his brother, which were over the treasures of the house of the LORD" — and the witnesses take it as the settling of the first trust before the record turns to the second. The reformed John Gill notes plainly that Zetham and Joel were both sons of Jehieli, the two on whom the charge fell.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, confirm the appointment and then use this verse as the hinge into the next section. Zetham and Joel, the heads of the house of Jehieli which sprang from Laadan the Gershonite, were over the treasures of the house of the LORD; and with their oversight the household treasury is settled. The record now, they observe, moves to the Kohathite side — "of the Amramites, and the Izharites, the Hebronites, and the Uzzielites," the four chief branches of the Kohathite family — for it is from that stock, and not from the Gershonites, that the keepers of the dedicated things will be drawn, Shebuel the son of Gershom being set as prince over the treasures. The Amramites, Izharites, Hebronites, and Uzzielites, they note, are the four chief branches of that Kohathite family, gathered at 1 Chr 23:15 and following, and it is among them, not among the Gershonite Laadanites, that the guardianship of the consecrated spoils is henceforth to be sought.

The witnesses do not divide, and the near-repetition of v.21 is not idle. Where the previous verse traced the pedigree of the Gershonite treasurers, this one fastens their names and their office beyond doubt — Zetham and Joel, over the treasures of the house of the LORD — as though the Chronicler would leave no ambiguity about who answered for the ordinary stores of the sanctuary. Gill's brief identification and Keil and Delitzsch's pivot meet on the same structural sense: the first treasury is now closed and sealed under the Gershonite house of Jehieli, and the account is free to open the second, the treasury of the consecrated spoils, under the Kohathite descendants of Moses. Two families, two trusts, named in turn; and the seam between them falls exactly here, at the confirmed appointment of Zetham and Joel.
23:
The verse lists the four Kohathite clans — "of the Amramites, and the Izharites, the Hebronites, and the Uzzielites" — and the witnesses read it as a heading thrown up over all that follows. The reformed John Gill names their descent from the four sons of Kohath — Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel — and at once notices what is missing: the account will treat of all these families in their concerns with the treasures and the outward business, all, that is, but the Uzzielites, who are never afterward mentioned. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sees the same structure, calling the enumeration a sort of heading to the rest of the chapter, which relates to the Amramites at verses 24 through 28, the Izharites at v.29, and the Hebronites at verses 30 to 32, drawing on the fuller roll of 1 Chr 23:12.

The absence of the Uzzielites draws the witnesses' comment. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley offers two reasons: either the family was now extinct — which would explain why we read no more of it in Scripture but only here and at Numbers 3:27 — or else there was none of them at that time fit to be employed and trusted in these matters. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, simply register the four branches as the Kohathite families from whom the following stewards and officers are taken.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement quietly illustrates the Chronicler's method. He sets down the full and symmetrical list of four houses, and then follows only three of them, letting the fourth fall silent — whether because it had died out or because it had no man ready for the trust. Gill and Ellicott map the heading onto the sections it governs; Wesley supplies the likely reason for the gap; and between them they show that the record is honest to its own materials. It does not invent an Uzzielite officer to round out the pattern, but names the house and passes it by, so that the reader sees both the ideal frame of the four Kohathite clans and the real history in which one of them, for whatever cause, has no part to play in the ordering of David's temple.
24:
A single name lights up the treasury-list — "Shebuel the son of Gershom, the son of Moses, was ruler of the treasures" — and the witnesses feel the weight of a descendant of Moses holding office at last. The reformed John Gill marks it as the first time any of Moses's posterity is noticed in a place of honour, authority, and trust, and reports that Jarchi and Kimchi made Shebuel the chief over all the treasures and treasurers under him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the rank: the office of comptroller-in-chief was hereditary in this Amramite house, and Shebuel is therefore called "ruler," or rather prince — nagid, the word used at 1 Chr 5:2 — both departments of the treasury named at v.20 being subject to his control.

Over the man's identity the witnesses divide. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, following the Targum, holds that Shebuel is none other than Jonathan — the wandering Levite of the book of Judges — who "returned to God," which the name Shebuel is taken to mean, and whom David, seeing him expert in money matters, constituted his chief treasurer. Gill knows the tradition and rejects it, judging the Targum to identify Shebuel with that Jonathan "very wrongly." So a real disagreement stands: was this treasurer the reclaimed idol-priest of a darker story, brought at last into honest service, or simply an otherwise unknown descendant of Moses, wrongly draped by legend in Jonathan's name?

Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, keep to what the text plainly gives — Shebuel the son of Gershom, the son of Moses, ruler of the treasures — and pass on to his kinsmen of the line of Eliezer. The witnesses converge, whatever they make of the Targum's romance, on the striking fact the verse records: that the descendants of the great lawgiver, who might have claimed the highest place, are found not among the priests at the altar but here, as stewards over the temple's wealth, entrusted with its money and its dedicated spoils. Whether or not Shebuel was Jonathan reclaimed, his appointment sets Moses's own seed in a post of sober fidelity, guarding the treasures of the house that the law of Moses had commanded Israel to build.
25:
The verse traces a second strand of Moses's line — "his brethren by Eliezer" — and the witnesses agree that its whole point is to arrive at a name still to come. The reformed John Gill reads it as a genealogy of Shebuel's kinsmen who descended, not through Gershom, but through Eliezer the other son of Moses: Rehabiah his son, and Jeshaiah, and Joram, and Zichri, and Shelomith, each begetting the next in an unbroken succession down to the last.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott states the purpose outright: the object of the verse is to show the extraction of Shelomith, whose function will be defined in v.26. These men are called the "brethren" of Shebuel because, like him, they spring from Moses — Shebuel through Gershom, they through Eliezer — so that the two branches are cousins within the one house of the lawgiver. Ellicott is careful, too, to keep this Shelomith distinct: the Amramite Shelomith of Eliezer's line is not to be confused with the Gershonite Shelomith of 1 Chr 23:9, nor with the Izharite of the next chapter's courses. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, give the identical succession — Rehabiah, Jeshaiah, Joram, Zichri, Shelomith — and explain that these descendants of Eliezer are named brethren of Shebuel precisely because both alike came down from Moses.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse, dry as it looks, does a necessary work. It is a bridge of five names laid down for the sake of the sixth, so that when the record says at v.26 that "Shelomith and his brethren were over all the treasures of the dedicated things," the reader already knows exactly who this Shelomith is — a great-great-grandson of Moses through Eliezer, cousin to the chief treasurer Shebuel, and no other of the several men who bore the common name. Gill supplies the succession, Ellicott the purpose and the caution against confusion, Keil and Delitzsch the reason for the word "brethren"; and together they show the Chronicler doing what a careful register must — establishing a man's descent before entrusting him, in the reader's sight, with the consecrated wealth of the house of God.
26:
Now the man so carefully traced is given his charge — "which Shelomith and his brethren were over all the treasures of the dedicated things, which David the king... had dedicated" — and the witnesses set out the second treasury and the great names behind it. The reformed John Gill identifies him as the Shelomith last named in the line of Eliezer, the younger son of Moses, now made keeper of the consecrated gifts. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, are careful to distinguish this Shelomoth from the Jisharite of 1 Chr 23:18 and the Gershonite of 1 Chr 23:9, and lay out the ranks of the dedicators: David the king, the chief fathers, the princes over thousands and hundreds — these being the war-captains — and the commanders-in-chief, men such as Abner and Joab. They fix the grammar too, reading "and the princes" where the received text has "to the princes," after the pattern of 1 Chr 29:6.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verse the same way — "he, Shelomoth, and his kinsmen" over the dedicated things — and corrects the same small slip, noting that "the captains over thousands" should be "the captains," a scribe's error, and pointing forward to the two captains of the host named at v.28, Abner and Joab, whose deeds are told at 2 Samuel 8:16 and 1 Chr 18:15.

The witnesses do not divide; they build together a picture of the second fund and its provenance. Where the ordinary stores of the house lay under a Gershonite hand, these dedicated treasures — the offerings of kings and captains, wrung from the wars of Israel — were laid under a descendant of Moses and his kinsmen. Gill fixes the man, Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott fix the grammar and the roll of donors, and all agree on the dignity of the trust: that the spoils vowed to God by David and his mightiest soldiers were not left to any chance keeper, but committed to a named house of proven descent, answerable for every consecrated thing. The treasury of the dedicated is thus shown to be as carefully manned as the gates themselves, a guard of stewardship set over the wealth that war had won and piety had given to the LORD.
27:
An explanatory clause tells where the dedicated wealth came from — "out of the spoils won in battles did they dedicate to maintain the house of the LORD" — and the witnesses gather round both the custom and its theology. The reformed John Gill notes that the temple was not yet built when these spoils were vowed; they were dedicated to raise it when its time should come, and to repair it and furnish its sacrifices after. He reaches for the parallel among the nations, recalling how the Romans laid up the best of their spoil in the temple of Jupiter Feretrius after the example of Romulus their first king, and sometimes built or adorned temples out of the plunder of war.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke enlarges that comparison. The custom of giving God a share of the spoils, he says, prevailed among almost all peoples who acknowledged a supreme Being, for all believed that victory came through him; and he cites Virgil, who has his heroes invade the welcome prey and call the gods, and Jove himself, to a share of it, with the note of Servius that the Romans, going to war, would promise part of the booty to their deities, so that there stood at Rome a temple of Jupiter the Spoiler. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the sacred lesson the pagans only shadowed: these dedicated things were given mostly out of the spoils of battle, as Abraham gave Melchizedek the tenth of his, as Moses's officers brought their oblation in Numbers 31:50; and the principle is that the more God bestows upon us the more he expects, so that men of war must honour God with their spoils, and great successes call for proportionable returns.

The witnesses part only over a word. What does it mean to dedicate spoil "to maintain" the house? The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the same verb means "to repair" or "restore" at 2 Kings 12:8, but judges the sense here rather "to make strong," to keep the house in strength — and Keil and Delitzsch read it likewise, "to preserve the house of the LORD in good condition." John Wesley, more simply, glosses it "or, to repair it." The difference is slight, between endowing the house and mending it, and the witnesses do not quarrel over it. On the great matter they are one: that the wealth of God's house was the sanctified overflow of Israel's wars, a tithe of victory laid up against the day the temple should rise, and a standing witness that whatever God gives to the strong he means, in part, to receive back in worship.
28:
The roll of dedicators is resumed and named — "all that Samuel the seer, and Saul the son of Kish, and Abner the son of Ner, and Joab the son of Zeruiah, had dedicated" — and the witnesses read it as evidence of a long, settled piety of war. The reformed John Gill glosses the first name, taking Samuel's dedicated spoil to be won in his battle with the Philistines, when the prophet judged Israel and the LORD thundered upon their enemies.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott treats the verse as picking up the thread from v.26 and glosses the ancient title: "the seer" is the older word for prophet, as the note in 1 Samuel 9:9 expressly explains. The closing phrase "and whosoever had dedicated any thing," he says, points to a general prevalence of the practice — the same devotion seen when David dedicated the spoils of his wars at 2 Samuel 8:11 — and indeed the Law itself ordained the dedication of all captured metals to the endowment of the sanctuary, as at Numbers 31:22 and Joshua 6:19. These slowly gathered spoils, Ellicott adds, from Samuel's day down to David's, help explain how so much wealth stood ready for the building and adorning of the temple, as at 1 Chr 22:14; and the whole store lay "under the hand of Shelomith," the same phrase of trust used of the singers.

The witnesses do not divide, and a quiet marvel rises out of the list. Here in one treasury lie the offerings of a prophet, a rejected king, a rival general, and a violent captain — Samuel and Saul, Abner and Joab, men of very unequal record — each of whom, in his hour of victory, had given something back to God. Gill fixes Samuel's spoils to the Philistine war; Ellicott shows the custom rooted in the Law and running through the whole history; and together they let the reader see that the wealth of the house of God was not the gift of the good alone. Even Saul, even Joab, had dedicated of their spoils, so that the treasury Shelomith kept was a kind of sediment of Israel's entire past, the accumulated acknowledgment, by saints and sinners alike, that the battle and its plunder belonged in the end to the LORD.
29:
The chapter turns from the sanctuary to the streets — "of the Izharites, Chenaniah and his sons were for the outward business over Israel, for officers and judges" — and the witnesses weigh both what this "outward business" was and what dignity it carried. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives it his fullest treatment. Now that every office of the house of God is provided, he says, here is an account of Levites employed as officers and judges in the outward business, which must not be neglected, no, not even for the temple itself; for the magistracy is an ordinance of God for the good of the church as truly as the ministry is. These Levites administered justice alongside the princes and elders, who could not understand the law so well as the men who made it their study; and none who served in the sanctuary, no singer or porter, was concerned in this outward work, one duty being enough to engage the whole man.

The witnesses divide over the phrase itself. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, following Jarchi, takes "the outward business" quite literally — work done outside the city: the cutting of timber, the hewing of stones, the ploughing of the fields belonging to the sanctuary. But the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott defines it rather as the office of "officers," that is scribes, and judges — the six thousand Levites set apart for these duties at 1 Chr 23:4 — and thinks it may in part have concerned the temple, including the collection of tithes and taxes, as the like phrase at Nehemiah 11:16 suggests. John Wesley agrees with the judicial sense, taking these for judges over the people in the several cities, entrusted with the law because the common law of Israel simply was the law of God, of which the Levites were the established interpreters.

So a genuine question stands: was the "outward business" the manual labour of the sanctuary's estates, as Clarke and Jarchi held, or the civil and judicial administration of Israel, as Henry, Ellicott, and Wesley maintain? The weight of the witnesses falls on the latter. Henry crowns it with a reflection: happy is the kingdom whose civil and sacred interests are thus interwoven and jointly advanced, so that between the Levites who served religion and those who served the state, both God and the king were well served. The chapter, having guarded the gates and secured the treasures, now sends the sons of Levi out into the common life of the nation, to see the law of God done in its courts as faithfully as its worship was done in its courts of stone.
30:
The record posts the first company of judges west of the Jordan — "of the Hebronites, Hashabiah and his brethren, men of valour, a thousand and seven hundred" — over the business of the LORD and the service of the king. The reformed John Gill does the arithmetic that ties the verse to the whole: supposing the Izharites of v.29 to have been sixteen hundred, these seventeen hundred, with the twenty-seven hundred beyond Jordan, make up exactly the six thousand officers and judges of 1 Chr 23:4. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, fix the sense of the key word: the term rendered "officers" means oversight or superintendence — the Septuagint's "for the inspection of Israel" — and they reject Bertheau's attempt to make it mean a fixed tribute, a meaning it cannot be shown to bear.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses expound the two spheres of the charge. Adam Clarke distinguishes them cleanly: "in all the business of the LORD" covers everything ecclesiastical, "in the service of the king" everything civil, so that here were established courts of both sacred and secular judicature; and he draws the abiding lesson that religion and the state should mutually support each other, the state ruling by the laws and the Church living by the Bible. John Wesley fills in the work: to see that the monies given toward the temple were faithfully gathered and sent up to Jerusalem, and that God's laws were executed among the people, while in the king's service these Levites were his eyes to see the people's transgressions and his hands to inflict due censure.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds a shrewd inference from a single word. The text sets these Hebronites over Israel "on this side Jordan westward," but the Hebrew idiom is properly "on the other side," the western bank being so named at Joshua 5:1 and Joshua 22:7 by writers standing to the east of the river. The use of that expression here, Ellicott suggests, seems to imply that the source on which the Chronicler drew was itself written somewhere east of the Jordan — perhaps at Babylon, in the exile. The witnesses do not divide on the substance: seventeen hundred Hebronites under Hashabiah, set to oversee the western tribes in the double interest of God and the crown; but Ellicott's geographical hint opens, in passing, a window onto the very desk from which the record may have come.
31:
The last officer is named and dated — "among the Hebronites was Jerijah the chief... in the fortieth year of the reign of David they were sought for" — and the witnesses attend to the man, the moment, and the place. The reformed John Gill identifies Jerijah as chief among the Hebronites, the posterity of Hebron the son of Kohath. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, explain that David set this branch of the Hebronites, under their head Jeriah, over the tribes east of the Jordan, and that a parenthesis is inserted here to tell why: the Hebronites were sought out, according to their generations and fathers, in the fortieth year of David's reign, and valiant men were found among them at Jazer of Gilead — a Levite city in the territory of Gad, assigned to the Merarites at Joshua 21:39.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens on the date and its poignancy. The fortieth year, he notes, was David's last, in which he made all the orders of families and officers recorded in these chapters — and he draws the lesson home: we should be so much the more diligent in doing good as we see the day approaching, and if we live not to enjoy the fruit of our labours, let us not grudge it to those who come after us.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gathers a quiet solemnity from its timing. This whole elaborate ordering of the temple's guards and treasurers and judges — the roster of ninety-three porter-heads, the two treasuries, the six thousand officers — was the work of a dying king's last year, when David, near his end, searched out the fittest men and set the house of God and the kingdom in order for the son who would build the temple he could not. Gill fixes the lineage, Keil and Delitzsch the reason and the place of the search, Wesley the moral of the deathbed labour; and together they let the reader feel that these dry registers are the final act of a life, David spending his fortieth year not in ease but in provision, that the worship and the government of Israel should stand firm when he was gone.
32:
The chapter closes east of the Jordan — "his brethren, men of valour, were two thousand and seven hundred chief fathers, whom king David made rulers over the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh" — and the witnesses read the last verse as the capstone of the whole administrative design. The reformed John Gill notes that the brethren and kinsmen of Jerijah the Hebronite were so many principal men in their families, men of fortitude and courage, set as heads over the trans-Jordan tribes. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, give the plain sense in full: these twenty-seven hundred chief fathers were made rulers over Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, "for every matter pertaining to God, and affairs of the king" — the same twofold charge, sacred and civil, that governed their brethren in the west.

The witnesses do not divide, and the closing verse quietly completes the arithmetic and the argument together. With the seventeen hundred Hebronites of v.30 set over the western tribes and these twenty-seven hundred over the eastern, the six thousand officers and judges of 1 Chr 23:4 are fully accounted for, and every part of Israel — both banks of the river — is brought under the double oversight of God's matters and the king's. That the larger company should be posted beyond Jordan is itself telling, for those remoter tribes lay furthest from Jerusalem and nearest the heathen frontier, most exposed to idolatry and most in need of Levites to guard both their faith and their order.

So the roll of the temple servants ends where it is fitting it should — not at the altar but at the borders, with valiant men of Hebron sent to the far tribes to keep the law of God and the peace of the king. Gill fixes their character, Keil and Delitzsch their commission; and between them they seal the chapter's whole vision, in which the ordering of David's last year reaches from the gates of the sanctuary out to the edges of the land. The porters guarded the doors, the treasurers kept the wealth, and now the Hebronite fathers hold the frontier, so that the service of God, begun at the threshold of his house, is carried to the outermost bounds of his people.
